authors: Dinghua Zhan;Yalin Zhang;Yang Yuan;Fatemeh Mayvaneh;Yunquan Zhang
title: Prenatal exposure to ambient formaldehyde and reduced fetal growth: A nationwide birth cohort study
abstract:
•The first birth cohort examining the nexus between ambient formaldehyde (HCHO) exposure and low birth weight (LBW) and small for gestational age (SGA). •Each 1 × 1015molec.cm−2increase in pregnancy-long HCHO was associated with an excess odds of 6.4%–8.5% for LBW and 5.6%–7.7% for SGA. •HCHO-associated risks were more pronounced among socioeconomically disadvantaged mothers (e.g., younger, less educated, and rural). •We observed robust evidence for synergistic interactions between HCHO and low greenness, elevated PM2.5and O3pollution. 
 BackgroundDespite accumulating evidence linking air pollution with adverse birth outcomes, the nexus between ambient formaldehyde (HCHO) and fetal growth remains insufficiently characterized in large-scale population studies.MethodsA nationwide cohort of nearly 4 million singleton live births in Iran from 2013 to 2018 was analyzed. Pregnancy-long average ambient HCHO concentrations were estimated from satellite-based tropospheric vertical column densities at 0.1° × 0.1° resolution. Multivariable-adjusted logistic models were used to estimate concentration–response relationships and odds ratios (ORs) for low birth weight (LBW) and small for gestational age (SGA). We assessed additive interactions between HCHO and co-exposures (greenness and air pollution) and decomposed the overall burden into components attributable to individual and joint exposures.ResultsThreshold-free and super-liner associations were observed for both LBW and SGA, with steeper risk increases at higher HCHO concentrations >12.5 × 1015 molec.cm−2. Each 1 × 1015 molec.cm−2 increase in prenatal HCHO was associated with an elevated risk of LBW (OR = 1.078, 95% confidence interval [CI]: 1.075–1.081) and SGA (OR = 1.070, 95% CI: 1.068–1.072). Stronger effects were observed in socioeconomically disadvantaged subpopulations of younger, less-educated, and rural mothers. Significant synergistic interactions were consistently identified between HCHO pollution and low greenness and elevated levels of PM2.5 and O3, with the greatest joint effect and attributable burden observed for HCHO-greenness co-exposure. Approximately 31266 (95% CI: 30143–32274) LBW cases and 54580 (95% CI: 53290–55858) SGA cases were attributable to combined exposure to high HCHO and reduced greenness, representing 12.20% (95% CI: 11.78%–12.61%) and 11.61% (95% CI: 11.33%–11.88%) of totally observed adverse birth outcomes, respectively.ConclusionsThis nationwide analysis provided novel evidence linking prenatal ambient HCHO exposure to an elevated risk of LBW and SGA, and we observed synergistic associations of HCHO exposure and reduced greenness and elevated levels of air pollution.

authors: Jing Cao;Jiahui Kang;Wen Xu;Ming Chang;Wenkai Lei;Yifei Lu;Yuepeng Pan
title: Atmospheric deposition of dissolved carbon into Lake Erhai, China
abstract:
•Dissolved Organic Carbon (DOC) deposition inputs to a plateau lake were estimated. •Potentially bioavailable DOC from wet deposition exceeds riverine inputs 3-fold. •Myanmar biomass burning contributes atmospheric DOC deposition into Lake Erhai. 
 This study quantified the wet deposition of dissolved organic carbon (DOC) and dissolved inorganic carbon (DIC) over 1 year at seven sites within the Erhai Lake Basin, providing the first estimates of atmospheric DOC inputs to plateau lake watersheds. Annual wet deposition fluxes varied between sites, ranging from 8.6 to 12.4 kg C ha−1 yr−1 for DOC and from 3.6 to 6.5 kg C ha−1 yr−1 for DIC. The atmospheric deposition of DOC directly entering Erhai Lake was quantified at 11 kg C ha−1 yr−1, with bioavailable DOC (BDOC) inputs (167 t yr−1) surpassing riverine BDOC fluxes by a factor of three. The concentrations of DOC and DIC exhibited pronounced seasonal and spatial variability, peaking during winter and spring, with annual volume-weighted mean (VWM) values of 1.48 mg C L−1 for DOC and 0.73 mg C L−1 for DIC. Correlation analyses revealed biomass burning, fossil fuel combustion, and mineral dust as primary DOC sources, whereas mineral dust was the dominant source of DIC. Source apportionment revealed that local emissions and long-range transport (notably spring and winter biomass burning from northern Myanmar) were the major contributors. These findings highlight transboundary transport and atmospheric deposition as critical pathways of dissolved carbon input to plateau lakes, with significant implications for carbon modeling and ecosystem impacts.
